title: The Antwerp Diamond Heist: Beaten by a Sandwich
description: In February 2003, a crew of Italian thieves emptied 109 safe deposit boxes beneath the most secure building in the world's diamond capital, without tripping a single alarm. They beat a three-ton vault door, seismic sensors, and a lock with one hundred million combinations. Then a half-eaten salami sandwich brought the whole thing down.

sources.text: Joshua Davis, 'The Untold Story of the World's Biggest Diamond Heist,' Wired, March 2009
Scott Andrew Selby and Greg Campbell, 'Flawless: Inside the Largest Diamond Heist in History,' Union Square Press, 2010
BBC News coverage of the February 2003 Antwerp Diamond Centre theft and the 2005 trial of Leonardo Notarbartolo

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Okay, wait, before you start. Someone asked me this week what our show is even about, and I said, it's about people who almost got away with it.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Almost being the whole show.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: The almost is the show! Nobody makes a podcast about the ones who got away clean.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Because we never learned their names.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Okay see, that's genuinely creepy, and now I'm going to think about it all night. What did you bring me?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: I brought you a vault. Antwerp, Belgium. February 2003. Two floors beneath the city's Diamond Centre: a three-ton steel door, a combination dial with one hundred million possible codes, a seismic sensor, a magnetic alarm, infrared heat detectors, and a light sensor. The diamond district around it has its own private police force. Cameras on every corner.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Sounds cozy.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: On the Monday after Valentine's weekend, the guards opened that vault and found one hundred nine safe deposit boxes torn open and picked clean. Diamonds, gold, cash, jewelry. The estimates start at one hundred million dollars and go up from there.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: I'm sorry, a hundred million? And nobody heard anything? Not one alarm?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Not a beep. No broken door, no tunnel. The vault was simply... opened.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Okay, yeah, I'm in. I'm Ruby, she's Frankie, and this is Two Grifters.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Today: the Antwerp diamond heist. The crew who spent two years planning the perfect job, executed it almost flawlessly, and then lost everything on the drive home. Because of a sandwich.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: [laughs] Every time you say it I love it more.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Our main character is Leonardo Notarbartolo. Italian, from Turin, in his fifties. Silver hair, nice suits, easy smile. If you met him you'd guess retired architect, maybe a jeweler.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And what was he actually?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: A career thief. He'd been stealing since he was a kid, and he ran with a loose crew of Turin specialists the Italian press later called the School of Turin. Not muscle. Craftsmen. Alarm guys, lock guys, key guys.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: A trade school for burglars.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Basically. And in the year 2000, Notarbartolo rents himself a small office inside the Antwerp Diamond Centre. Puts his name on the door as a diamond merchant. Pays about seven hundred dollars a month.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Hold on. He just... rented an office in the building he was going to rob?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: For more than two years. And that rent came with something wonderful: a tenant ID card, twenty-four-hour access to the building, and his very own safe deposit box down in the vault.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: So the world's most secure building handed the burglar a key to the front door and a locker in the safe.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: They also waved at him. Every day. The guards knew him. He was polite, he was boring, he was invisible. And the whole time, he was watching everything.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: This is the long game. Two years of hello-how-are-you.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: There's a detail I think about a lot. He kept a pen camera. Little spy camera in his shirt pocket. Every trip to his box, he'd film a bit more. The door. The sensors. The routines. Then he'd go home to Turin and the crew would study the footage like game film.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Wait, they rebuilt it, didn't they. Tell me they built a practice vault.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: A full-scale replica. Somewhere near Turin, the crew constructed a copy of the vault room and rehearsed in it. Over and over, in the dark.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: That is a movie. That is literally the montage from every heist movie.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Except the movies copied reality. This is where the montage comes from.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: So let's talk about what they were up against, layer by layer. Because every layer got beaten, and every solution is embarrassingly simple.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: My favorite genre.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Layer one. The vault door. Three tons of steel, a hundred million combinations, plus a foot-long key. Drilling it would take days and wake up the seismic sensor.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: So how do you open a door like that?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: You film it. Investigators believe a tiny camera hidden above the vault door recorded the concierge dialing the combination. And the key? The crew found where the building stored it and made a copy. One member was so good at this the others called him the King of Keys.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: The King of Keys. Incredible job title. Terrible retirement plan.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Layer two. The magnetic alarm on the door. Two plates that scream the moment they separate. The crew came in the night before, unscrewed the plates while the field was still connected, taped them together on a strip of aluminum, and just... set them aside. Still connected. Still happy. Telling the alarm company everything was fine.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Shut up. They didn't beat the sensor, they just, what, moved it? And it kept phoning home saying everything's fine here?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: With hardware-store tape. Layer three, the infrared heat detector inside the vault. Walk in front of it and your body heat trips it.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Hairspray. They misted the sensor with a film of hairspray to blind it just long enough, then mounted a polystyrene shield over it. Body heat never reaches the sensor.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Hairspray? Like the can in my bathroom right now? That hairspray?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Layer four, the light sensor: tape over it. Layer five, the cameras: bags over them. Layer six, the box doors themselves. Solid little steel doors with copper keyholes, rated to stand up to serious tools.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Let me guess. Something from a garage.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: A homemade puller. A drill bit, a wrench, and a slide weight. Crank it into the keyhole, yank, and the door pops in about a minute. They opened a hundred nine boxes that way, by hand, in the dark, wearing headlamps with the lenses half covered.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: A minute a box. They rehearsed that in the replica, didn't they.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Until they could do it by feel.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: February fifteenth, 2003. Saturday night, Valentine's weekend. The diamond district is empty. Most of the dealers are gone, the neighboring building has a big party covering the noise, and the crew lets themselves in through a side entrance with a garage.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Using the credentials the building gave their pal two years ago.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: They're inside the vault around midnight. Notarbartolo stays outside as lookout with a radio. The crew works the boxes for hours, sweeping everything into satchels. Loose diamonds, gold bars, watches, stacks of currency, entire parcels of stones waiting to be traded on Monday.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: How much could they even carry?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: That's the thing, they ran out of ability to carry it. They left boxes unopened. There was too much treasure.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Too much treasure. A problem exactly zero people have ever had.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: By early Sunday morning they're out clean, back at Notarbartolo's rented apartment, sorting the take on the floor. Alarm never sounded. Nobody knows anything until Monday.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: So at this point they've committed the largest diamond theft in history and the world hasn't noticed yet.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: For one more day, it is the perfect crime.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Now. Rule one of the perfect crime: leave nothing behind. The crew knows this. So all the evidence, the tools, the tape, the videotape from the hidden camera, the receipts, the wrappers from their lunch, gets bagged up to be burned back in Italy.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Reasonable plan.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: One member of the crew, Pietro Tavano, nicknamed Speedy, is driving the getaway route with Notarbartolo when he panics. He's convinced they're being followed. He cannot stand having the evidence in the car.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Oh no. Speedy, no.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: They pull off the highway near a village called Vilvoorde, and Speedy hurls the garbage into the brush along a muddy track next to the E19. Half in a ditch, scattered in the weeds.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: In Belgium. They dumped the evidence in the country they robbed.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Twenty minutes from the crime scene. And here enters my favorite person in this entire story: August Van Camp. A retired grocer who owns that little strip of woods and patrols it like a national park.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: [laughs] Of course he does.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Van Camp finds the trash on his land, and he is furious about the littering. He pokes through it, sees envelopes printed with the words Antwerp Diamond Centre, and calls the police to complain.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: He reported the crumbs of the crime of the century as illegal dumping.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The police almost brushed him off. Then somebody connected the address to the weekend's news. In those bags: videotape of the vault, tools, Belgian francs, envelopes from looted boxes, and a half-eaten salami sandwich.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And a sandwich is DNA.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: A sandwich is DNA. It matched Leonardo Notarbartolo. And a supermarket receipt in the bag led to security footage of the man himself, buying the crew's lunch.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: He beat a hundred-million-combination lock and got caught by cold cuts and a loyalty receipt.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The building's records showed his tenant badge swiping in at odd hours. The perfect tenant became the obvious suspect overnight.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Police arrest Notarbartolo when he comes back to Antwerp. In court, Belgium gives him ten years. Three accomplices from Turin, Ferdinando Finotto, Elio D'Onorio, and Speedy Tavano, get five years each.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And the King of Keys?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Never identified. Never caught. To this day.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Somewhere out there is an elderly Italian man who can open anything, and no one knows his name.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Now for the part that keeps this story alive. The diamonds. The loot. Over one hundred million dollars' worth.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Recovered?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Essentially none of it. A few stones and some cash in the trash bags, a little more in Notarbartolo's apartment. The rest has never surfaced. Not at trial, not since.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: A hundred million dollars is just... out there.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: And Notarbartolo, from prison, gave one interview, to a writer from Wired magazine, and told a wild version of events: that a diamond dealer hired the crew, that the vault was half empty because the whole thing was an insurance job, and that the real take was far smaller.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Do we believe him?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Investigators don't. Accounts genuinely differ on the details, and he's a professional liar with a decade to polish the story. But nobody has ever proven where the diamonds went.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: So the scoreboard reads: vault, zero. Sandwich, one.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The most secure room in Europe was beaten by tape, hairspray, and patience. And the most careful crew in Europe was beaten by one man who couldn't sit with a bag of trash for two more hours.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: The real vault was the friends we annoyed along the way.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: That's the Antwerp diamond heist. Next time on Two Grifters: a man who sold the Eiffel Tower. For scrap. Twice.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Twice! He got them twice. Until then: lock your boxes, and never, ever litter.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Two Grifters is an independent show. When the record disagrees with itself, we say so on air, and the sources for every episode are in the show notes.
